claim: For all integers n > 1, the sum of divisors sigma(n) is at least n + 1, with equality exactly when n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, n is composite if and only if sigma(n) > n + 1.
For all integers n > 1, the Euler totient phi(n) is at most n - 1, with equality exactly when n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, sigma(n) + phi(n) is at least 2n, with equality if and only if n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if sigma(n) + phi(n) = 2n.
For all integers n > 1, the number of divisors tau(n) is at least 2, with equality if and only if n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if sigma(n) + phi(n) = n * tau(n).
If n is prime, then the sum of divisors of n^2 is n^2 + n + 1.
If n is prime, then the Euler totient of n^2 is n^2 - n.
If n is prime, the sum of sigma(n^2) and phi(n^2) is 2n^2 + 1.
If n is prime, the number of divisors of n^2 is exactly 3.
For any prime n, the identity sigma(n^2) + phi(n^2) = n^2 * tau(n^2) is false.
If n is a Mersenne prime 2^p - 1, then sigma(n) is a power of 2.

Every even perfect number is of the form 2^{p-1}(2^p - 1) where 2^p - 1 is prime.

For prime p, the sum sigma(p^2) + phi(p^2) is always odd.
